module_title: Authentication with the Microsoft identity platform

choices.prompt: According to the module, the Microsoft identity platform implements two open standards. Which standard is responsible for AUTHENTICATION (proving who a user is), as opposed to authorization?
choices: OpenID Connect
OAuth 2.0
SAML 2.0
JSON Web Token (JWT)

choices.prompt: The Northwind expense portal is a server-side confidential web app that signs a human employee in to read their own calendar. Per the module's decision rule, which OAuth 2.0 flow should it use?
choices: Authorization code flow
Client credentials flow
Authorization code flow with PKCE and no secret
Implicit flow

choices.prompt: A Graph call returns a 403 in the Northwind portal. Based on the module, what is the most likely cause?
choices: A missing or unconsented scope on the access token
The access token was sent for the wrong audience (aud claim)
The client secret was committed to source control
The app hard-coded the /common authority instead of the tenant ID
correct_answers: A missing or unconsented scope on the access token

choices.prompt: Which statements about delegated versus application permissions are correct according to the module? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Delegated permissions mean the app acts as the signed-in user and can do only what that user is allowed to do.
Application permissions mean the app acts as itself with no user and typically apply tenant-wide.
Application permissions usually require an administrator to consent.
Delegated permissions are the right choice for an unattended daemon with no signed-in user.
correct_answers: Delegated permissions mean the app acts as the signed-in user and can do only what that user is allowed to do.
Application permissions mean the app acts as itself with no user and typically apply tenant-wide.
Application permissions usually require an administrator to consent.

choices.prompt: Per the module, what does an app registration in Entra ID provide to give an application its own identity? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A client ID, described as the application's public username
One or more redirect URIs where tokens may be returned
Credentials such as a client secret, certificate, or federated credential
The signed-in user's password, stored securely for later reuse
correct_answers: A client ID, described as the application's public username
One or more redirect URIs where tokens may be returned
Credentials such as a client secret, certificate, or federated credential

choices.prompt: According to the module, what is the distinct purpose of an ID token versus an access token?
choices: An ID token answers "who signed in?" and is consumed by your app to establish a session, while an access token answers "what is this caller allowed to do against a specific resource?" and is sent to an API.
An ID token is sent to Microsoft Graph in the Authorization: Bearer header, while an access token is kept private and never leaves your app.
An ID token grants tenant-wide application permissions, while an access token grants only delegated per-user permissions.
An ID token is a client secret used to authenticate the app, while an access token is the user's password presented to the resource.
correct_answers: An ID token answers "who signed in?" and is consumed by your app to establish a session, while an access token answers "what is this caller allowed to do against a specific resource?" and is sent to an API.

choices.prompt: Per the module, a teammate stores a client secret inside a single-page app's JavaScript so it can call Graph. Why is this wrong, and what should they do instead?
choices: A secret in a SPA's JavaScript is public to anyone with dev tools; SPAs must use the authorization code flow with PKCE and no secret.
A secret in a SPA is fine as long as it is also stored in Key Vault; they should add Key Vault and keep the secret in the JavaScript.
The problem is the wrong audience on the secret; they should request a Graph-scoped secret instead of a backend-API secret.
The secret should be replaced by the user's ID token, which the SPA can safely embed and send to Graph instead.
correct_answers: A secret in a SPA's JavaScript is public to anyone with dev tools; SPAs must use the authorization code flow with PKCE and no secret.

choices.prompt: The module warns about hard-coding the /common authority for a single-tenant internal line-of-business portal. According to the module, what is the consequence and the correct alternative?
choices: Using /common allows any Microsoft account to attempt sign-in; an internal portal should target its specific tenant ID so only that organization's identities are accepted.
Using /common causes every Graph call to return 403 because no scopes are consented; the fix is to switch to application permissions.
Using /common forces the implicit flow instead of the authorization code flow; the fix is to add a client secret to the authority URL.
Using /common silently disables token expiry, so tokens never refresh; the fix is to enable MSAL's token cache.
correct_answers: Using /common allows any Microsoft account to attempt sign-in; an internal portal should target its specific tenant ID so only that organization's identities are accepted.

choices.prompt: According to the module, which statements about access tokens, their audience, and token lifetime are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A token's aud claim binds it to one resource, so acquiring a token for your own API and sending it to Graph yields a 401.
Access tokens expire (commonly around an hour), so you should use MSAL's token cache and refresh tokens to renew silently rather than re-running the full interactive flow each request.
Because tokens are signed by Entra ID and scoped to a single audience, a token minted for one API is useless against another.
Once issued, an access token is valid indefinitely and can be reused against any Microsoft API regardless of audience.
correct_answers: A token's aud claim binds it to one resource, so acquiring a token for your own API and sending it to Graph yields a 401.
Access tokens expire (commonly around an hour), so you should use MSAL's token cache and refresh tokens to renew silently rather than re-running the full interactive flow each request.
Because tokens are signed by Entra ID and scoped to a single audience, a token minted for one API is useless against another.

choices.prompt: Based on the module's description of Microsoft Graph and how a resource validates a presented token, which statements are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Microsoft Graph is the unified REST API for Microsoft 365 data such as users, groups, calendars, mail, files, and Teams, called at https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/...
The scopes requested on the token (for example User.Read or Calendars.Read) determine which Graph endpoints succeed, and the scope on the token must line up with the permission on the registration.
The resource validates the token's signature, issuer, audience, and expiry, and never sees a password.
Microsoft Graph requires a separate password for each endpoint, which the app must store and send alongside the access token.
correct_answers: Microsoft Graph is the unified REST API for Microsoft 365 data such as users, groups, calendars, mail, files, and Teams, called at https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/...
The scopes requested on the token (for example User.Read or Calendars.Read) determine which Graph endpoints succeed, and the scope on the token must line up with the permission on the registration.
The resource validates the token's signature, issuer, audience, and expiry, and never sees a password.

llm.prompt: A nightly daemon service at Northwind runs with no user present and must read all employees' mailboxes tenant-wide. Explain which permission type and OAuth flow you would use and why, and contrast this with how the per-user expense portal handles permissions.
llm.reference_answer: The daemon should use application permissions with the client credentials flow, because there is no signed-in user, so the app must act as itself rather than as a delegate. Application permissions apply tenant-wide and are designed for unattended daemons, but because they are far more powerful they usually require an administrator to consent. The interactive expense portal, by contrast, has a human in the loop, so it uses delegated permissions and the authorization code flow, acting as the signed-in user and able to do only what that user is allowed. Choosing delegated whenever a user is present is the principle of least privilege made concrete; granting Calendars.Read as an application permission to a per-user portal would over-grant by letting it read everyone's data.

llm.prompt: Walk through how the Northwind confidential web app uses MSAL and the authorization code flow to acquire a token and call Microsoft Graph to read the signed-in employee's calendar, and explain why the code never sees the user's password.
llm.reference_answer: First the app is registered in Entra ID to obtain a client ID, tenant, redirect URI, and a client secret, which are stored as environment variables rather than in source. Using MSAL's ConfidentialClientApplication, the app calls get_authorization_request_url to send the browser to Entra ID's sign-in page for the Calendars.Read scope. The user authenticates and consents at Entra ID, which redirects back to the redirect URI with an authorization code; the callback handler calls acquire_token_by_authorization_code to exchange that code plus the client secret for an access token. The app then calls https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/events with the token in the Authorization: Bearer header, and /me resolves to the signed-in user precisely because the token is delegated. The code never sees the password because authentication happens entirely at Entra ID, which returns a signed token instead of any credential.

llm.prompt: Compare an ID token and an access token as described in the module, and explain why a token minted for one API is useless against another and what symptom appears if you send a token to the wrong resource.
llm.reference_answer: An ID token answers 'who signed in?' and is consumed by your own app to establish a user session, while an access token answers 'what is this caller allowed to do against a specific resource?' and is sent to an API like Microsoft Graph in the Authorization: Bearer header. Both are signed JSON Web Tokens issued by Entra ID, and the resource validates the token's signature, issuer, audience, and expiry rather than ever seeing a password. A token is bound to a single audience by its aud claim, so a token minted for one API is useless against another because the second resource will reject a token whose audience does not match it. Concretely, if you acquire a token for your own backend API and send it to Graph, the call fails with a 401; the fix is to request scopes for the resource you actually intend to call.
